title: Kubernetes Pods
learningObjectives: Write Pod manifests with container specs, resource limits, and probes
Use init containers and sidecar containers
Configure liveness, readiness, and startup probes
Set resource requests and limits for containers

theory.content:
Pod Specification

A Pod spec defines one or more containers, volumes, and pod-level settings. Containers in a Pod share the same network namespace (IP, port space) and can share volumes.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: web-app
  labels:
    app: web
spec:
  containers:
    - name: nginx
      image: nginx:1.27
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
      resources:
        requests:
          memory: "64Mi"
          cpu: "250m"
        limits:
          memory: "128Mi"
          cpu: "500m"
      livenessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /healthz
          port: 80
        initialDelaySeconds: 10
        periodSeconds: 30
      readinessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /ready
          port: 80
        initialDelaySeconds: 5
        periodSeconds: 10

Resource Requests and Limits

Requests: The minimum resources guaranteed for scheduling. The scheduler uses requests to find a node with sufficient capacity.

Limits: The maximum resources a container can use. Exceeding CPU limit causes throttling; exceeding memory limit causes OOMKill.

Probes

  • livenessProbe: Container is alive? Failure → restart container
  • readinessProbe: Container is ready to serve traffic? Failure → remove from Service endpoints
  • startupProbe: For slow-starting containers; delays liveness/readiness probes until startup completes

Init Containers

spec:
  initContainers:
    - name: wait-for-db
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ['sh', '-c', 'until nc -z db-service 5432; do sleep 2; done']
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: myapp:latest

Init containers run to completion before app containers start. They are commonly used to wait for dependencies, run migrations, or set up configuration.
